coredata.dc:title: A hydrothermal route to multicolor luminescent carbon dots from adenosine disodium triphosphate for bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Ming
Chi, Cheng
Yuan, Ping
Su, Yutian
Shao, Maoni
Zhou, Ninglin
coredata.dc:description: In this work, phosphor and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (N-P-doped CDs) were developed for bioimaging. The as-synthesized N-P-doped CDs emit a bright blue coloured fluorescence after exposure to a 365nm UV-lamp illumination. It is also demonstrated that the fluorescence of CDs is resistant to the interference of metal ions, saline solution, and high ionic strength environments. The bright fluorescence nature of the N-P-doped CDs has proven them to be excellent probes for cellular imaging. And this guess is further confirmed by using a laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM). The viability study was carried out by MTT assay, suggesting the high biocompatibility of N-P-doped CDs. The results demonstrated that N-P-doped CDs may be considered as a safe material for bio-imaging and drug delivery in cancer cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bioimaging
Biocompatibility
Drug delivery
Cancer therapy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic illustration of the possible mechanism for the formation and the application of N-P-doped CDs using ATP as the carbon source.
(A) TEM of N-P-doped CDs. Insets are HR-TEM image and particle size distribution of N-P-doped CDs. (B) XRD pattern of N-P-doped CDs. XPS survey scan (C) and N 1s (D) of N-P-doped CDs.

(A) EDS of N-P-doped CDs. (B) FT-IR spectra of N-P-doped CDs. (C) UV−vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence excitation, and emission spectra of the N-P-doped CDs dispersed in water at room temperature. The inserts show the photograph and fluorescence image of the N-P-doped CDs solution under UV light of 365nm (right). (D) Fluorescence response of N-P-doped CDs at different pHs (pH1–9). The inserts show the photograph and fluorescence image of the N-P-doped CDs at different pH.

(A) Comparison on the photobleaching characteristics of N-P-doped CDs and FITC under a 500W xenon lamp. (B) Effect of UV exposure time on the fluorescence intensity of the N-P-doped CDs. (C) Fluorescence response of N-P-doped CDs in the presence of different concentrations of NaCl, (I0 and I correspond to the fluorescence intensity of N-P-doped CDs in the absence and presence of NaCl, respectively.). (D) Fluorescence response of N-P-doped CDs in the presence of different metal ions (50μM).

(A–D) Confocal images of HeLa cells observed under blue, red, and overlay channels. Cells were incubated with 500μg/mL N-P-doped CDs medium for 3h and cell nuclei were stained using DAPI. All the scale bars are 20μm. Viability of HeLa cells after incubation with in N-P-doped CDs medium for 24h (E) and 48h (F). The error bars represent variations among three independent measurements.
Hemolysis rate (A) and recalcification times (B) of ATP and N-P-doped CDs at different concentrations.

head.ce:title: A hydrothermal route to multicolor luminescent carbon dots from adenosine disodium triphosphate for bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center for biological functional materials, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Nanjing Normal University, Nanjing 210023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center for biological functional materials
College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Biofunctional Materials, Jiangsu Engineering Research Center for Biomedical Function Materials, Nanjing 210023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Biofunctional Materials
Jiangsu Engineering Research Center for Biomedical Function Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanjing Zhou Ninglin Advanced Materials Technology Company Limited, Nanjing 211505, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanjing Zhou Ninglin Advanced Materials Technology Company Limited

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center for biological functional materials, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Nanjing Normal University, Nanjing 210023, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center for biological functional materials
College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, phosphor and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (N-P-doped CDs) were developed for bioimaging. The as-synthesized N-P-doped CDs emit a bright blue coloured fluorescence after exposure to a 365nm UV-lamp illumination. It is also demonstrated that the fluorescence of CDs is resistant to the interference of metal ions, saline solution, and high ionic strength environments. The bright fluorescence nature of the N-P-doped CDs has proven them to be excellent probes for cellular imaging. And this guess is further confirmed by using a laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM). The viability study was carried out by MTT assay, suggesting the high biocompatibility of N-P-doped CDs. The results demonstrated that N-P-doped CDs may be considered as a safe material for bio-imaging and drug delivery in cancer cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) emerged as a new kind of fluorescent carbon nanomaterial, have received much attention due to their high photostability against photobleaching, no blinking, biocompatibility, low toxicity, and environmental friendliness [1–3]. Therefore, they have been successfully used in bioimaging, catalysis, and sensing [4–5]. Carbon dots with good biocompatibility and small diameter are easy to enter into the cells. Nanoparticles with properties suitable for multi-imaging have received significant attention and can be used in multifunctional composite systems. Extensive effort has been devoted to design nanocarriers that combine cell targeting drug storage, controlled drug release capabilities [6,7], detection and isolation of cancer cells.

Nowadays, many different kinds of inorganic nanoparticles (NPs) have been used for drug and gene delivery, such as biomolecule, carbon dot (CDs), carbon nanotubes, gold and silica nanoparticles [8–9]. Recent experimental studies in rats indicate that inhaled carbon nanotubes may produce significant lung toxicity in rats, and the toxicity potential increases with decreasing particle size and increasing surface area [9]. As we known, gold and silica NPs need doped with fluorescent dyes to afford imaging capability. In addition, it remains challenging to synthesize stable, monodisperse, mesoporous silica NPs with a pore size larger than 5nm [8]. However, CDs are an appropriate candidate as an emerging bioimaging agent with excellent chemical inertness, biocompatibility and managed size for the entrance of mesopores [10]. Compared to previous carbon nanotubes, gold and silica NPs, [11] carbon dot delivery systems can be used for minimizing cytotoxicity, improving clinical outcomes and potential nano carriers for the next generation of multifunctional nanomedicine [12].

Nucleotide is the basic unit of nucleic acid and works as one of the most vital substances in human body, like conditioning cellular morphology, metabolic activities and protein biosynthesis. The naturally occurring nucleotide adenosine 5′-triphosphate (ATP) is normally present in every living cell of the human body and is well-known for its role in intracellular energy metabolism [13]. Adenosine triphosphate (ATP) is the universal energy currency in cells, containing cyclic carbon rings, nitrogen, phosphorus, and oxygen atoms [14]. Currently, the nucleotide adenosine-5′-triphosphate (ATP) was used as the nitrogen and phosphorus precursor to produce PN-CDs through a simple one-step hydrothermal method [15].

Herein, for the first time, we report that the water-soluble carbon dots are prepared from ATP. The PL quantum yield of N-P-doped CDs reaches 9.8%; the mean diameters of N-P-doped CDs are 3.8nm in the optimal conditions. XRD analysis indicates that the N-P-doped CDs are amorphous. FTIR spectroscopy was used to characterize the surface groups of the N-P-doped CDs. Moreover, the carbon dots exhibites excellent fluorescence properties, high photostability, and low cytotoxicity.

Adenosine Disodium Triphosphate was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All the other chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received. Double-distilled water (18.2MΩ) was obtained from Milli-Q system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA) and used in the experiments. Adult whole blood was provided by Jiangsu blood centre. Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) was purchased from Thermo Fisher HyClone (USA). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was obtained from SunShine Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (China). Methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium (MTT) was purchased from Amresco (USA). The human cervical cancer cells (HeLa cells) and human pulmonary adenocarcinoma cell (A549 cells) were purchased from the Cell Bank of Culture Collection of Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China).

N-P-doped CDs were prepared as follows: Firstly, ATP (0.5g) was dissolved in 20mL deionized water to form a transparent solution under magnetic stir in 50mL beaker. Secondly, the transparent solution was transferred into a 50mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave followed by hydrothermal treatment at 180°C for 10h, then cooled to room temperature naturally, and the color of the solution turned from colorless to yellow which indicated the formation of N-P-doped CDs. Thirdly, the obtained coarsely yellow solution was filtered by a 0.22μm syringe filte to remove the black residue. Fourthly, the clear yellow solution obtained in the second step was centrifuged at 12,000rpm for 15min to remove large suspended particles. Finally, The obtained N-P-doped CDs solution was dried to a powder by vacuum freeze-drying machine. In addition, lyophilized yellow powder was collected and stored under 4°C before further characterization and applications study [10].

The photoluminescent (PL) spectra was recorded using molecular fluorescence spectrometer (Cary Eclipse, varian, USA) while ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra was recorded using UV absorption spectrophotometer (Cary-50, varian, USA). The Malvern ZEN 3600 Zetasizer (Malvern Insruments, UK) was used to study the Zeta potential. The morphologies of the samples were characterized using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, H-7650, Hitachi, Japan). The fluorescent images of cells were acquired by laser scanning confocal microscope (TI-E-A1R, Nikon, Japan). The surface composition and element analysis of the samples were recorded using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, EscaLab-250, Thermo, USA). FTIR spectra of the samples was recorded using Nexus670 of Nicolet, USA. XRD pattern of the samples was recorded using D/MAX-rC of Neo-Confucianism, Janpan. Proton nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy was tested by 400MHz WB Solid-State NMR Spectrometer, Switzerland.

The quantum yield (QD) was a main parameter that used for characterizing the optical conversion efficiency of fluorescent material. Generally, the quinine sulfate as a reference substance was used to measure the quantum yield. Specific experimental procedure are as follows: firstly, a small amount of the quinine sulfate (Φ=54%) was dissolved in 0.1M solution of H2SO4, and the N-P-doped CDs were dissolved in deionized water. Then, the absorbance values of N-P-doped CDs and quinine sulfate were measured at 360nm. Ensuring both of the absorbance values were less than 0.05. In addition, the integral fluorescence intensity of N-P-doped CDs and quinine sulfate were obtained by measuring both of PL emission spectra at an excitation wavelength of 360nm. Finally, the quantum yield of N-P-doped CDs was calculated using following equation:
Φ =
Φ st
K x
/
K st
η x
/
η st
2
The experimental procedures were consisted with the published paper [16].

We assessed vitro cytotoxicity of ATP and N-P-doped CDs by the MTT assay in HeLa cells. At first, the HeLa cells were cultivated in culture flask which containing DMEM (High Glucose) replenished with 10% fetal bovine serum, 1% penicillin and streptomycin mixed solution at 37°C filling with 5% of CO2. The HeLa cells were lifted with 1mL trypsin-EDTA when cells covered approximately 80% confluence in the flask. Then, the HeLa cells were seeded into 96-well culture plates with 100μL cells suspension. Culturing overnight, discarding the original DMEM of 96-well culture plates, the solutions of ATP and N-P-doped CDs with concentrations at 0.05, 0.1, 0.25, and 0.5mg/mL were added to each wells, respectively, each of which was repeated in triplicate. Being incubated at 37°C for 24h and 48h, respectively, then each well was added to 20μL MTT and incubated for an additional 4h, the medium of each well was depleted and 100μL dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was added to each well to generate bluish violet formazan. Finally, We immediately measured the optical density (OD) of each well at 570nm on BioTek synergy 2 Multi-Mode Microplate Reader (BioTek, USA) to assess the cell viability.
Cell inhibition rate =
1 −
D t
/
D b
× 100 %
where Dt is the absorbance of sample; Db is the absorbance of blank control group [17].

The A549 cells were cultivated in culture flask which containing DMEM replenished with 10% fetal bovine serum, 1% penicillin and streptomycin mixed solution at 37°C filled with 5% of CO2 in incubator. Then the A549 cells were seeded into 35-mm Petri dish after the cells were lifted with 1mL trypsin-EDTA. The 35-mm Petri dish containing the A549 cells was put in incubator at 37°C filled with 5% of CO2 for whole night. Next, the 35-mm Petri dish was added to fresh medium (containing 0.5mg/mL of N-P-doped CDs) after removing obsolete DMEM containing 10% fetal bovine serum, 1% penicillin and streptomycin mixed solution. The A549 cells were cultivated for 0.5h in incubator before cell imaging, and the A549 cells were treated with phosphate buffer saline (PBS, pH=7.4) for three times to wash the unabsorbed free N-P-doped CDs after removing the medium. Finally, using paraformaldehyde to fix with the cells for at least 10min, and then the cells imaged on laser scanning confocal microscope [18]. For DAPI (4′, 6-diamidino-2-phenylindole) staining experiments, before imaging by CLSM, cells were first incubated with DAPI (Invitrogen) for 10min.

Solutions with different concentrations of N-P-doped CDs (10, 20, 40, 60, 80, and 100μg/mL) were prepared with normal saline (0.9% sodium chloride) and kept at 37°C for 30min. The fresh rabbit whole blood was separated by centrifugation at 1500rpm for 10mins, and the supernatant was carefully removed. The subnatant red blood cells (RBC) were washed with PBS. Then, the diluted RBC suspensions (0.2mL) was systematically added to 10mL of different concentrations of N-P-doped CDs solutions. These mixed samples were incubated for 3h at 37°C, and then centrifuged at 1500rpm for 10min after incubation. The supernatant was transferred to a 96-well plate where the absorbance was measured at 545nm using BioTek synergy 2 Multi-Mode Microplate Reader. The mean value of three measurements was calculated. As a positive controls, 0.2mL of diluted RBC suspensions was added to 10mL of deionized water, and as negative controls, 0.2mL diluted RBC suspensions was added to 10mL of normal saline solution. Hemolysis degree was calculated as:
Hemolysis rate =
D t
−
D nc
/
D pc
−
D nc
× 100 %
where ODt, ODnc and ODpc were the absorbance values of the test samples, negative control group and positive control group, respectively.

The N-P-doped CDs used adenosine disodium triphosphate (ATP) as the sole material source by a simple hydrothermal process at 180°C for 10h. The ATP could achieve single-step to prepare N-P-doped CDs without additional reagent of modification because the structure contained N and P. Although a detailed mechanism about the formation process of CDs remained unclear, it is proposed that the formation of N-P-doped CDs was likely to proceed via four stages with summarizing the previous reports [19–20]. As shown in Fig. 1 , the ATP firstly occurred decomposition at high temperatures after hydrolysis, then the decomposed products by aggregation and condensation to produce different soluble polymeric products [21]. The small dots grew at high temperature with the continuous carbonization and finally formed N-P-doped CDs nanocrystalline. The surface of lattice structures of N-P-doped CDs connected with a vast amount of hydroxy, carboxyl and amino groups that endowed the N-P-doped CDs with charming photoluminescence and excellent water dispersibility. Moreover, fluorescence of the N-P-doped CDs aqueous solution showed multicolor imaging of blue, green and red under different excitation wavelengths by cultivating with A549 cells. So, the as-prepared N-P-doped CDs were promising candidates for cellular imaging and drug delivery.

As shown in Fig. 2A, TEM images showed the formation of N-P-doped CDs with an average diameter of 3.8nm (150 measured particles). The HR-TEM of N-P-doped CDs showed the presence of amorphous carbon. Similarly, the XRD pattern of the N-P-doped CDs showed a broad reflection peak at 2θ=23° (Fig. 2B), consisting with the image of HR-TEM. Although the diffraction peak was broad, the resulting N-P-doped CDs were highly crystalline. Based on calculation from Bragg equation (2dsinθ=nλ), the interlayer spacing distance of N-P-doped CDs was to be 0.38nm where d was interlayer spacing distance of the N-P-doped CDs and λ was approximately 0.154nm. The chemical composition of N-P-doped CDs were further characterized by SEM combined with EDS analysis and XPS. Similarly, the XPS full scan spectrum (Fig. 1C) showed four obvious peaks ascribed to C, O, N, and P. The survey X-ray photoelectron spectral (XPS) scan of N-P-doped CDs, revealed that N-P-doped CDs were mainly composed of carbon, phosphorus, nitrogen, and oxygen the binding energy peaks at 531.1, 396.7, 285.4, 197.5, 131.3eV, 104.5eV, 836.4eV, and 851.6eV corresponding to O 1s, N 1s, C 1s, P 2s and P 2p, respectively. Fig. 2D displayed the N 1s spectrum which could be deconvoluted into four peaks at 397.5, 398.2, 399.0, and 399.6eV, representing N 1s states in C-N-C, N-(C)3 functionalities, pyridinic-like N, and pyrrolic-like N, respectively. The P 2p peak in Fig. S1 depicted P 2p3/2 and PC binding motifs of N-P-doped CDs. As shown in Fig. 2A, the spectrum of EDS exhibited characteristic peaks of C, O, N, and P, further confirming the dope of N and P.

Moreover, the functional group at the surface of N-P-doped CDs was studied by FTIR spectra analysis. Some differences existed between CDs and ATP, as shown in Fig. 2B. In the FTIR spectrum of N-P-doped CDs, the wide absorption peaks at 1289cm−1 were attributed to stretching vibration of PO and a stretching vibration band of OH with a broad peak at 3500cm−1. Being compared with the FTIR of ATP, the disappeared absorption peaks at 1677cm−1 and 2388cm−1 suggested that ATP may be mostly carbonized during hydrothermal process. Also CH at 2950cm−1, CC at 1438cm−1 were observed in the ATP and N-P-doped CDs. Similarly, the structure of N-P-doped CDs could be investigated by NMR (Fig. S2) spectra. P NMR spectra showed that the phosphate group remained in the N-P-doped CDs. Therefore, N-P-doped CDs could be stabilized in aqueous solution mainly because as it contained a large amount of hydrophilic groups (amidogen, carboxy, and hydroxyl) on the surface, and those hydrophilic groups could provide further hydrophilic reactions and emited charming photoluminescence. And the zeta potentials of N-P-doped CDs were −12mV (Fig. S3), indicating the advantage of the electrostatic interactions with antineoplastic drug (such as doxorubicin) [22].

The synthesized N-P-doped CDs Showed excitation and emission peaks at 340nm and 440nm, respectively (Fig. 3C). Similar to other CDs [23–24], showed a sharp absorption peak at 280nm. The absorption peaks at 250nm and 340nm were possibly due to the π–π* transition of the conjugated CC band [25]. The photographs of N,P-C-dots in aqueous solution displayed bright blue fluorescence (inset of Fig. 3C). In addition, the PL emission spectrum of the N-P-doped CDs was excited at various excitation wavelengths, which was shifted from 430 to 455nm (red-shift of 25nm) upon excitation wavelengths from 310 to 380nm of 10nm increments (Fig. S4A, B). Its maximum intensity first increased as excitation wavelength changed from 330 to 340nm, and then dropped from 340 to 380nm. The intensity of PL emission increased to maximum at 340nm. Therefore, the synthetic N-P-doped CDs also possessed excitation λex-dependent PL behaviors like previous CDs [26]. The λex-dependent PL behavior was useful for multicolor in vitro biological imaging applications. And the fluorescent QY upon excitation at 340nm was calculated to be 9.8% by using the quinine sulfate as a reference substance. Similar to other carbonaceous nanomaterials, N-P-doped CDs have a mass of chemical functional groups on the basal plane and on their edges. These chemical structures facilitate the fast recombination of electron–hole pairs from the π and π* electron levels of sp2 clusters, thus either inducing fluorescence or enhancing fluorescence intensity. Based on its FTIR spectrum (Fig. 3A) and the UV–visible absorption spectrum (Fig. 3C), N-P-doped CDs emitted blue or longer-wavelength PL, resulting from excited electrons relaxing back to either the sp2 energy levels or defect states by π–π* transition of the conjugated CC band [27–28]. Consequently, dots which have had more defects induced by doping N and P elements onto their surface gain more fluorescence than standard CDs.

As a basic elements of the external solution environment, pH played an significant role that affected the PL intensity of N-P-doped CDs on the external solution environment. So, the pH effect on the fluorescence intensity of N-P-doped CDs was investigated (Fig. 3D). It was found that the fluorescence intensity of N-P-doped CDs did not change obviously when pH was 3–9, which demonstrated that the synthesized N-P-doped CDs could apply in imaging of tumor cells. As presented in Fig. 4A, after irradiation for 60min, the FL intensity of the CDs could still reserve 88%, whereas the FL intensity of FITC decreased to only 27% of its original value. The photostability of the N-P-doped CDs was also investigated via exposing the N-P-doped CDs suspension to a UV lamp (365nm). We found that no obvious decrease in fluorescence intensity under continuous UV irradiation (Fig. 4B). Interesting, the N-P-doped CDs exhibited well stability even the NaCl concentration was 1mol/mL (Fig. 4C), indicating that the prepared N-P-doped CDs could work at higher salt concentration. The FL characteristics of N-P-doped CDs solution in the presence of a variety of metal ions that may exist in living cells and some heavy metal ions were also studied, as shown in Fig. 4D. Metal ions did not show obvious interference on the FL intensity. The above experiments indicates that the N-P-doped CDs prepared in this work are featured with outstanding pH- and photostabilities, high resistance to photobleaching, and inertness to interference of salt concentration and metal ions, suggesting them to be a promising candidate for bioimaging applications under physiological conditions [29].

The N-P-doped CDs labeled A549 cells after incubation with 0.3mg/mL for 5h at 37°C, the fluorescent images of different excitation wavelengths were acquired by LSCM. It was clearly observed that after 0.5h of incubation was incubation the intensity of the red fluorescence which appeared in the N-P-doped CDs treated cells was higher compared to the cells treated without N-P-doped CDs (Fig. S5). The cytoplasm of A549 cells around the cytoplasm were clearly emerged red fluorescence under 543nm excitation wavelengths (Fig. 5B). The nuclei of A549 cells did not show any fluorescence, suggesting that either N-P-doped CDs molecules did not migrate into the nuclei at all or that fluorescence was completely quenched by the nuclei. The quenching by nuclei was highly unlikely and thence it suggested N-P-doped CDs had a much higher binding affinity to the cytoplasm than the nuclei. To further confirm the localization of the N-P-doped CDs inside the cells, we stained the cell nuclei with DAPI (Fig. 5A, blue channel). We observed that the N-P-doped CDs were preferentially located at the cytoplasm and nucleolus (Fig. 5C, overlay) as detected by confocal microscopy. The uptake of these N-P-doped CDs by the A549 cells could be similar to the pathway for some CDs, which penetrate into the cell membrane by disturbing the structure and conformation of the lipid bilayer [30–31]. The charming fluorescence and extraordinary biocompatibility of N-P-doped CDs were very appropriate for biomedical applications, especially fluorescence imaging of cell labeling and fluorescent tracer of drug delivery.

Notably, N-P-doped CDs are highly biocompatible as shown by cytotoxicity experiments. As shown in Fig. 3F and G, the viability of HeLa cells were unchanged within 24h and 48h when the concentration of N-P-doped CDs was 50, 100 and 250μg/mL, as compared with the blank group. We further tested the toxicity and feasibility of N-P-doped CDs as bioimaging agents in plants. The selected plants were raised in aqueous solution of N-P-doped CDs. The plants exhibited characteristic blue luminescence under 365nm UV irradiation (Fig. S6), illustrating that it could permeate throughout the plant cells but did not hinder plant growth.

Hemolysis of the blood usually is considered to be evaluated bioincompatibility of various biomedical materials. Hemolysis caused the release of hemoglobin from RBCs, and indicated the disturbance of RBCs membrane integrity. The hemolysis value under 5% was regarded as biosafety of biomedical materials [32]. As shown in Fig. 6A, the hemolysis experiments were evaluated the blood compatibility of the ATP and N-P-doped CDs with different concentrations solutions. In this case, these results showed that all of concentrations with the ATP and N-P-doped CDs possessed favourable blood compatibility because all of hemolysis value were less than 5%. And the hemolysis rate in the presence of the N-P-doped CDs was lower than the raw material ATP. Therefore, the N-P-doped CDs possessed preferable blood compatibility than ATP by a simple one-step hydrothermal process.

Coagulation is the culmination of a series of reactions, ultimately resulting in the thrombin-catalyzed transformation of fibrinogen into an insoluble fibrin clot [33]. Thrombin is formed upon the convergence of the intrinsic and extrinsic pathways of coagulation. Viewed from the perspective of blood-material interactions, the intrinsic pathway is triggered by surface contact and causes a linear cascade of reactions, requiring Ca2+ ions to participate. As a result, plasma recalcification profiles serve as a measure of the intrinsic coagulation system. The absorbance increases as the plasma becomes more muddy, correlating with the formation of a clot. Recalcification times of ATP and N-P-doped CDs complexes were shown in Fig. 6B. The average recalcification times were about 16–23min for different concentrations of N-P-doped CDs, which was significantly longer than that of pure plasma.

In conclusion, a λex-dependent PL behavior of N-P-doped CDs for biological imaging applications is developed. The high performance can be attributed to the dope of N and P. Moreover, N-P-doped CDs have shown a comparable or better biocompatibility than most previous CDs. And N-P-doped CDs can also be used for cell imaging by exploiting their luminescence. Meanwhile, the hemolysis test and recalcification times has inferred that the N-P-doped CDs possessed favourable blood compatibility and negligible toxicity for RBCs from volunteer. Thus, they might find potential applications in bio-imaging, drug delivery systems and biomedical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by the Jiangsu Six Category Outstanding Talent (2012-NY-031), Jiangsu Province Science and Technology Support Plan (BE2014327, BE2015367), the Jiangsu Agriculture Science and Technology Innovation Fund CX(15)1016, JHB05-21 and Nanjing-321, the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD 2016-2017).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2017.03.144.
